events.text: Patient is a 60-year-old female of White/Caucasian ethnicity, Eastern European descent, BMI 33.2, with a history of morbid obesity, obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine.
At age 59, patient presented with intermittent hematochezia and was found on colonoscopy to have a large polypoid mass in the ascending colon (C18.2).

Biopsy of the ascending colon mass revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (histology 8140/3) arising in a background of tubulovillous adenoma, with invasion into submucosa and muscularis propria.

Comprehensive genomic profiling identified KRAS G12D mutation, BRAF wild-type, PIK3CA Q546R co-mutation, TP53 G245S mutation, SMAD4 R361H mutation; microsatellite stable (MSS) with intact mismatch repair proteins; PD-L1 CPS 2; tumor mutational burden 5 mutations/Mb.

Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at age 59 showed ascending colon mass with ileocolic lymphadenopathy, five hepatic metastases (right lobe predominant, largest 3.9 cm in segment VII), and two left lower lobe pulmonary nodules (1.4 cm and 0.9 cm) consistent with metastases; staged T3N2aM1b.
PET/CT at age 59 confirmed FDG-avid ascending colon mass (SUVmax 12.8), hepatic lesions (SUVmax 6.3 to 9.1), and pulmonary nodules (SUVmax 4.2 and 3.1); no other sites of metastatic disease.
At age 59, baseline CEA was 112 ng/mL and CA 19-9 was 88 U/mL; right-sided primary with liver and lung metastases; not a surgical candidate at this time; palliative systemic therapy recommended.

At age 59, patient started first-line FOLFIRI (irinotecan 180 mg/m2, 5-FU, leucovorin) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg every 2 weeks, chosen over FOLFOX due to pre-existing mild neuropathy from bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome.
After cycle 2 of FOLFIRI-bevacizumab, patient developed grade 2 diarrhea and grade 2 nausea; managed with loperamide and dose reduction of irinotecan to 150 mg/m2.
At age 59, patient experienced grade 2 hypertension (BP 158/94) on bevacizumab, requiring increase of existing lisinopril and addition of hydrochlorothiazide.
Restaging CT after 6 cycles showed partial response with decrease in hepatic metastases (largest now 2.6 cm) and decrease in pulmonary nodules (now 0.8 cm and 0.5 cm); CEA decreased to 38 ng/mL.
At age 60, CA 19-9 decreased to 41 U/mL; good partial response to FOLFIRI-bevacizumab; continued on same regimen with dose adjustments.
At age 60, patient developed grade 1 proteinuria (urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 0.8) on bevacizumab; monitored without dose change.
CT at age 60 after 12 cycles showed continued partial response; hepatic metastases further decreased (largest 1.8 cm); pulmonary nodules near-resolved (3 mm and not visualized); CEA 14 ng/mL.
At age 60, given sustained response with low-volume residual hepatic disease, patient underwent laparoscopic right hemicolectomy to address the primary tumor.
Right hemicolectomy specimen showed residual moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with treatment effect, ypT3N1a (2 of 26 lymph nodes positive), negative margins, grade 2 tumor regression.

At age 60, resumed FOLFIRI-bevacizumab postoperatively; liver surgery deferred due to multifocal residual disease not amenable to safe resection; planned for continued systemic therapy with reevaluation.
At age 60, patient experienced grade 2 fatigue limiting daily activities; discussed treatment holiday versus continued therapy; patient elected to continue with supportive care.
Surveillance CT at age 60 showed stable residual hepatic lesions (largest 1.7 cm) and no recurrence at colectomy site; CEA stable at 11 ng/mL; CA 19-9 was 29 U/mL.
At age 60, patient maintained ECOG 1 performance status on FOLFIRI-bevacizumab maintenance; plan to continue until progression or intolerance with imaging every 3 months and CEA monitoring.

scenario_blurb: Stage IV colorectal cancer with KRAS G12D mutation. Left-sided and right-sided primary tumors. Treated with FOLFOX or FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab. Some with liver-limited metastases considered for hepatic resection. MSI/MMR testing performed.
